- EXPOSITION ONLY: The “quotients and inclusion” fact is nontrivial and should be cited/hypotheses checked. The needed result is the projective version: valuated matroid quotient iff the corresponding projective tropical linear spaces are included. Brandt–Eur–Zhang state this as Theorem 4.3.1, allowing projective tropical linear spaces and loops. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))
- EXPOSITION ONLY: The “uniqueness of Plücker vectors” fact is nontrivial and should be cited. A standard reference states that a tropical linear space determines its tropical Plücker vector up to global tropical scalar. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2003.02660))
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The codimension-one identification is central but not proved. The proof must show, for rank \(2\) and \(3\) modular simple \(M\), that \(q\mapsto \nu_q\) is exactly a bijection between \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) and rank \(r-1\) valuated quotients of \(M\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The assertion that same-rank inclusions are equal is not justified. It follows from the quotient theorem plus a short same-rank quotient argument, but that argument should be inserted.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
Insert the following lemmas.

**Lemma 1: Same-rank inclusions are equal.**  
Let \(v,w\) be valuated matroids of the same rank \(k\) on \(E\), and suppose \(L(v)\subseteq L(w)\). By the quotient/inclusion theorem, \(v\) is a valuated quotient of \(w\). The underlying ordinary matroids then form a same-rank matroid quotient, hence have the same bases. For adjacent bases \(B=I\cup\{a\}\), \(B'=I\cup\{b\}\), the equal-rank tropical incidence relation for \(I\) and \(J=I\cup\{a,b\}\) has exactly the two finite terms
\[
v_B+w_{B'},\qquad v_{B'}+w_B.
\]
Thus they are equal, so \(v_B-w_B=v_{B'}-w_{B'}\). Since the basis graph is connected, \(v-w\) is constant on all bases. Hence \(v\) and \(w\) define the same projective valuated matroid and the same tropical linear space.

**Lemma 2: Codimension-one quotients are identified with \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).**  
For \(r=2\), \(M=U_{2,E}\), and hyperplanes are atoms. Rank-one quotients are exactly points of \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\); the polarity just permutes coordinates, so the stated map is bijective.

For \(r=3\), let \(\nu\) be a rank-two valuated quotient of \(M\). First show \(\nu_{ij}\) depends only on the line \(H=\operatorname{cl}(i,j)\). If \(a,b,c\) are distinct points on a line \(H\), choose \(d\notin H\). The quotient incidence relation with \(A=\{a\}\), \(J=\{a,b,c,d\}\) has only two finite \(M\)-terms, giving
\[
\nu_{ab}=\nu_{ac}.
\]
Varying \(a,b,c\) gives well-defined line weights \(w_H\). Define \(q_e=w_{e^\perp}\).

Now prove \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). For a line \(H\) and three distinct points \(a,b,c\le H\), let \(h=H^\perp\). Choose a line \(D\) not containing \(h\). By modularity, \(D\) meets \(a^\perp,b^\perp,c^\perp\) in distinct points \(u_a,u_b,u_c\). The quotient incidence relation for \(A=\{h\}\), \(J=\{h,u_a,u_b,u_c\}\) gives
\[
\min(q_a,q_b,q_c)
\]
attained at least twice. Thus all 3-point line circuits satisfy the tropical circuit condition. In a simple rank-three modular matroid, every circuit is either a 3-subset of a line or a 4-set with no three collinear; the 4-circuit condition follows from the line conditions: if \(a\) were the unique minimum in \(\{a,b,c,d\}\), then with
\[
x=\operatorname{cl}(b,c)\cap \operatorname{cl}(a,d),
\]
the line \(a,d,x\) forces \(q_x=q_a\), while the line \(b,c,x\) gives a contradiction. Hence \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).

Conversely, for \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\nu_{ij}=q_{\operatorname{cl}(i,j)^\perp}.
\]
The rank-two Plücker relations are checked by cases on a 4-subset \(J\). If \(J\) lies on a line, all three Plücker sums are equal. If \(J=\{a,b,c,d\}\) with \(a,b,c\) collinear and \(d\notin\operatorname{cl}(a,b,c)\), the Plücker relation becomes the tropical line-circuit condition for the three poles of \(ad,bd,cd\), which lie on \(d^\perp\). If \(J\) has no three collinear, use the flag-of-flats description of the Bergman fan: finite \(q\) is a nonnegative linear combination of characteristic vectors of a chain of flats. In rank three such a chain has length at most two, \(p\le L\). For each flat \(F\), count how many of the two side-poles in each opposite-side pair lie in \(F\). For a point \(p\), the count vector is \(0\) or a permutation of \((1,0,0)\); for a line \(L\), it is one of \(0\), a permutation of \((1,0,0)\), a permutation of \((2,0,0)\), or \((1,1,1)\). If \(p\le L\), the point contribution can be nonzero only in coordinates where the line contribution is already positive, so every positive linear combination has its minimum attained at least twice. This proves the remaining rank-two Plücker relation. The \(\infty\)-coordinate cases follow by closure/limits. The flag-of-flats characterization is the standard Bergman fan description. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2003.02660))

Finally, the quotient incidence relations for \(\nu\) are checked similarly: for \(A=\{x\}\) and a 4-set \(J\), the cases “all on a line,” “one collinear triple plus an outside point,” and “no three collinear” reduce respectively to automatic relations, equality of same-line weights, or the line-circuit condition for \(q\) on \(x^\perp\). Thus \(\nu\) is exactly a rank-two valuated quotient of \(M\), and the inverse is \(w_H\mapsto q_e=w_{e^\perp}\).
problem_solved: After inserting the missing codimension-one quotient identification and same-rank quotient lemma, the proof establishes the stated special-case theorem: for finite loopless simple modular matroids of rank at most \(3\) whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution, the relative Dressian of valuated quotients admits an inclusion-reversing involution extending the flat-lattice polarity.

w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{a\}}(x)+\mu\mathbf 1_B(x).

The remaining matching-cost argument in Lemma 4.1 is then valid also when \(\lambda\) or \(\mu\) is \(\infty\), since all costs are nonnegative extended-real costs and the conclusion is only “no unique finite minimum.”

After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed partial theorem: for a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\) with an order-reversing involution on its lattice of flats, every rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient satisfying the stated tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations is \(\infty\) on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and is constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The construction
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
\]
for \(M\)-independent \((r-k)\)-sets \(J\), and \(\infty\) otherwise, gives a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), descends to projective valuated matroids, is involutive, and sends the flat quotient \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the relative Dressian.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Same-rank quotient lemma.**  
   If \(v\) is a valuated quotient of \(w\) and both have rank \(k\), then their underlying ordinary matroids \(V,W\) form a same-rank quotient. Using the standard extension definition, there is a matroid \(N\) on \(E\sqcup X\) with \(N\setminus X=W\) and \(N/X=V\). Since \(r(V)=r(W)\),
   \[
   r_N(E\sqcup X)-r_N(X)=r_N(E).
   \]
   Hence \(r_N(E\sqcup X)=r_N(E)+r_N(X)\). By submodularity, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   r_N(A\sqcup X)+r_N(E)\ge r_N(E\sqcup X)+r_N(A)
   =r_N(E)+r_N(X)+r_N(A),
   \]
   while subadditivity gives the reverse inequality. Thus
   \[
   r_N(A\sqcup X)=r_N(A)+r_N(X),
   \]
   so
   \[
   r_V(A)=r_{N/X}(A)=r_N(A\sqcup X)-r_N(X)=r_N(A)=r_W(A).
   \]
   Therefore \(V=W\). Then the proof’s adjacent-basis argument applies: for adjacent bases \(B=I\cup\{a\}\), \(B'=I\cup\{b\}\), the quotient incidence relation gives
   \[
   v_B+w_{B'}=v_{B'}+w_B.
   \]
   Since the basis graph is connected by repeated basis exchange, \(v_B-w_B\) is constant on all bases, so \(v\) and \(w\) differ by a global tropical scalar.

2. **Rank-one elements are points of \(\Trop(M)\).**  
   A rank-one valuated matroid \(\eta\) has projective tropical linear space equal to the single projective point \([\eta_e]_{e\in E}\): pair-circuit equations force \(x_i-\eta_i\) to be constant on nonloops, and loop circuits force loop coordinates to be \(\infty\). Therefore a rank-one quotient \(\eta\) of \(\mu_M\) is equivalent, by quotient-inclusion, to the singleton \(L(\eta)\) lying in \(\Trop(M)\). Thus the rank-one part of \(\Dr(M)\) is canonically \(\Trop(M)\).

3. **Rank-three \(\alpha\)-identification details.**  
   In a simple rank-three matroid, circuits are exactly three-point subsets on a line and four-point subsets with no three collinear. In the proof that a rank-two quotient \(\nu\) gives \(q\in\Trop(M)\), the points \(u_a,u_b,u_c\) are distinct because
   \[
   x^\perp\cap y^\perp=(x\vee y)^\perp=H^\perp=\{h\}
   \]
   for distinct \(x,y\in H\), while \(u_x\ne h\). Also each \(\{h,u_y,u_z\}\) is a basis: \(u_y,u_z\in D\), \(h\notin D\), and \(u_z\notin y^\perp=\operatorname{cl}(h,u_y)\). Hence the quotient incidence relation really gives the tropical line-circuit condition for \(q_a,q_b,q_c\).

   For the Plücker check in the quadrangle case, one should insert the explicit case analysis. For a quadrangle \(\{a,b,c,d\}\), the six side-line poles \(s_i,s_i'\) are distinct. For any line \(L\), with \(\ell=L^\perp\), the vector
   \[
   m_L=\bigl(|L\cap\{s_1,s_1'\}|,\ |L\cap\{s_2,s_2'\}|,\ |L\cap\{s_3,s_3'\}|\bigr)
   \]
   can only be \(0\), a standard basis vector, twice a standard basis vector, or \((1,1,1)\): \(\ell\) lies on no side, one side, two opposite sides, or at a vertex of the quadrangle. If \(q=\lambda\mathbf 1_p+\mu\mathbf 1_L\) with \(p\le L\), then \(m_p\) is \(0\) or supported where \(m_L\) is positive. The three possible Plücker sums are the coordinates of \(\lambda m_p+\mu m_L\); in each of the above cases the minimum is attained at least twice.

   For quotient incidence when \(J\) has no three collinear, the duplicate-pole case should be handled explicitly. If a minimizing pole \(\operatorname{cl}(x,j)^\perp\) occurs at least twice, the minimum is repeated. If it occurs once, then among the remaining terms there are two distinct other poles; otherwise three elements of \(J\) would lie on a line through \(x\). These three distinct poles lie on \(x^\perp\), and the line-circuit condition for \(q\) forbids a unique minimum.

   Finally, for \(q\) with \(\infty\)-coordinates, use the definition of \(\Trop(M)\) as the closure of the finite Bergman fan. Choose finite \(q^{(n)}\to q\); the map \(q\mapsto \nu_q\) is coordinatewise continuous, and tropical Plücker/incidence conditions are closed because their failures are finite unions of strict-inequality open conditions.

4. **Incidence-symmetry equations.**  
   For a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\eta\), the circuit equations may be checked on the fundamental valuated circuits indexed by \((k+1)\)-subsets \(S\), with coefficients \(\eta_{S\setminus\{i\}}\). Thus, for \(k=r-1\), the equations over \(r\)-subsets used in Section 4 do cut out \(L(\nu_q)\). If \(S\) is a nonbasis line circuit of \(M\), all coefficients \((\nu_q)_{S\setminus\{i\}}\) are equal, possibly all \(\infty\), so the equation is either vacuous or exactly the ordinary circuit condition for \(p\in\Trop(M)\). Hence only basis equations remain, and the already-proved map \(B\mapsto B^*\) identifies them symmetrically.

5. **Coordinates of \(X_F\).**  
   If \(H\) is a hyperplane, then \(M/H\oplus U_{0,H}\) has rank one; its nonloops are precisely \(E\setminus H\). Therefore \(X_H\) is the point with coordinates \(\infty\) on \(H\) and \(0\) outside \(H\).  
   If \(a\) is an atom, then \(M/a\oplus U_{0,a}\) has rank \(r-1\). For a hyperplane \(K=\operatorname{cl}(I)\), the coordinate indexed by \(I\) is finite exactly when \(I\cup\{a\}\) is a basis of \(M\), equivalently \(a\nleq K\). Hence its hyperplane weight is \(\infty\) iff \(a\le K\), as used in Section 7.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Basis exchange inside a flat.**  
   If \(B,B'\) are bases of a flat \(F\), then they are bases of \(M|F\). By the basis-exchange axiom, if \(B\ne B'\), choose \(e\in B\setminus B'\); there exists \(f\in B'\setminus B\) such that \(B-e+f\) is again a basis of \(M|F\). This increases \(|B\cap B'|\). Iterating connects \(B\) to \(B'\) by single-element exchanges inside \(F\).

2. **Intervals and minors.**  
   For a flat \(Y\), the flats of \(M|Y\) are precisely the flats \(G\le Y\), so \(\mathcal L(M|Y)=[\hat0,Y]\). For a flat \(X\), the flats of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) correspond to flats \(G\) of \(M\) with \(X\le G\), so \(\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))=[X,E]\). Intervals in a modular geometric lattice are modular, so these minors/simplifications are finite simple modular matroids. If \(Y=X^\perp\), the polarity restricts to an order-reversing isomorphism
   \[
   [X,E]\longrightarrow[\hat0,Y],\qquad G\mapsto G^\perp .
   \]

3. **Restriction of \(p\) to \(Y\).**  
   In Section 8, if \(q_I=p_I\) for \(I\subseteq Y\), \(|I|=k\), and \(q\not\equiv\infty\), then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\): the tropical Plücker relations are inherited because all involved subsets lie in \(Y\), and circuits of \(M|Y\) are exactly circuits of \(M\) contained in \(Y\).

4. **Lifting circuits from contractions.**  
   Let \(Z\) be a flat with basis \(B\). If \(D\) is a circuit in \(\operatorname{si}((M|Y)/Z)\) and one chooses representatives \(e_X\in X\setminus Z\) for atoms \(X\in D\), then the representatives are dependent over \(Z\), while every proper subcollection is independent over \(Z\). Hence \(B\cup\{e_X:X\in D\}\) is dependent in \(M\), but \(B\) together with any proper subcollection is independent. Therefore any \(M\)-circuit contained in this set must contain every representative \(e_X\). This justifies the circuit-incidence applications in Lemma 5.1 and Lemma 7.1.

5. **Flat quotient computation.**  
   For \(N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\),
   \[
   r_{N_F}(A)=r_M(F\cup(A\setminus F))-r_M(F).
   \]
   Thus a set \(I\) of size \(n=r-r(F)\) is an \(N_F\)-basis iff \(r_M(F\cup I)=r\), equivalently \(F\vee \operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\hat1\). If \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) has rank \(n\), modularity gives
   \[
   F\vee G=\hat1
   \iff r(F\wedge G)=r(F)+r(G)-r=0
   \iff F\wedge G=\hat0.
   \]
   Hence \(\overline{p^F}(G)=0\) exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\), and \(\infty\) otherwise. Applying the polarity converts “\(G^\perp\) complements \(F\)” into “\(G\) complements \(F^\perp\),” proving \((p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}\).

6. **\(0/\infty\) Plücker vector.**  
   For an ordinary matroid \(N\), its basis indicator valuation \(v_B=0\) for bases and \(\infty\) otherwise satisfies the tropical Plücker relations. Indeed, after contracting the common \((m-2)\)-set in a three-term Plücker relation, the claim reduces to a rank-two matroid on four elements: if one perfect matching consists of two bases, the parallel-class relation prevents both other matchings from failing simultaneously. Thus the minimum cannot have a unique finite term.
problem_solved: After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed partial theorem: for a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) with an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\), every rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient satisfying the stated tropical Plücker and circuit-incidence relations is \(\infty\) on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and is constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The construction \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) on independent \((r-k)\)-sets gives a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), is projectively well-defined, is involutive, and sends \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) to \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the full relative Dressian.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Citation/convention insertion.** After stating the quotient-inclusion theorem, cite Brandt–Eur–Zhang, *Tropical flag varieties*, Theorem 4.3.1, with the convention that for valuated matroids \(v,w\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
L(v)\subseteq L(w)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{rk}(v)\le \operatorname{rk}(w)\text{ and }v\text{ is a valuated quotient of }w.
\]
Also cite Corollary 4.3.2(4) for the statement that a valuated quotient induces an ordinary matroid quotient on underlying matroids.

2. **Well-definedness of \(\alpha^{-1}\).** Insert after the rank-three bijection proof:
   
   If \(L(\nu)=L(\nu')\) are two rank-two quotient representatives of the same element of \(\Dr(M)_2\), then applying Lemma 1 to both inclusions gives \(\nu'=\nu+c\) on all bases. Hence their line weights satisfy \(w'_H=w_H+c\) for every line \(H\), so the recovered points \(q'_e=w'_{e^\perp}\) and \(q_e=w_{e^\perp}\) differ by the global scalar \(c\). Thus \(\alpha^{-1}\) is independent of the chosen representative. Conversely, if \(L(\nu_q)=L(\nu_{q'})\), Lemma 1 gives \(\nu_{q'}=\nu_q+c\). For each atom \(e\), choose a basis \(\{a,b\}\) of the line \(e^\perp\); then
\[
q'_e=(\nu_{q'})_{ab}=(\nu_q)_{ab}+c=q_e+c.
\]
So \(q'\) and \(q\) define the same projective point. Hence \(\alpha\) is genuinely a bijection on spaces.

3. **Rank-stratification insertion for Section 6.** Before the inclusion reversal argument, insert:
   
   By the full quotient-inclusion theorem, if \(L(v)\subseteq L(w)\), then \(\operatorname{rk}(v)\le \operatorname{rk}(w)\). The rank-zero projective tropical linear space is unique, giving \(\hat 0=X_E\). If \(L(v)\subseteq L(\mu_M)=\Trop(M)\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(v)=r\), then Lemma 1 gives \(L(v)=L(\mu_M)\), so the rank-\(r\) element is uniquely \(\hat 1=X_\varnothing\). Therefore, for \(r\le 3\), the only strict inclusions not involving \(\hat0\) or \(\hat1\) are point-in-rank-two inclusions in rank \(3\). This makes the case analysis exhaustive.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch for Lemma 3.1.**  
   After choosing \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimally with \(x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m\), insert: if the \(a_i\) were dependent, they would contain a circuit \(D\). For any \(d\in D\), \(d\in\operatorname{cl}(D\setminus\{d\})\), hence \(d\le\bigvee(D\setminus\{d\})\). Therefore removing \(d\) would not change the join, contradicting minimality. Thus \(a_1,\dots,a_m\) are independent.

2. **Patch for Lemma 4.1 flag normal form.**  
   After normalizing \(\min w=0\), choose \(\varepsilon>0\) smaller than every positive finite value of \(w\). Then
   \[
   A_\varepsilon=\{x:w_x\ge\varepsilon\}
   \]
   is a proper flat. If \(A_\varepsilon=\varnothing\), then all weights are \(0\). If \(A_\varepsilon\) is an atom \(a\), choose any hyperplane \(B\supset a\), set \(\beta=0\), \(\alpha=w(a)\). If \(A_\varepsilon\) is a hyperplane \(B\), then all atoms outside \(B\) have weight \(0\). Let \(\beta=\min_{x\in B}w_x\). If two distinct atoms of \(B\) had weight \(>\beta\), then for some real \(\tau>\beta\), \(A_\tau\cap B\) would contain two atoms of \(B\) but omit an atom of weight \(\beta\), contradicting flatness since two atoms in a rank-two flat span the whole hyperplane \(B\). Hence at most one atom \(a\in B\) has weight \(>\beta\); all atoms in \(B\setminus\{a\}\) have weight \(\beta\), and \(w(a)=\alpha\ge\beta\). This gives the displayed flag form.

3. **Patch for Lemma 7.1.**  
   Since \(\delta\) is an order-reversing isomorphism between rank-\(n\) geometric lattices, it sends rank \(s\) flats to rank \(n-s\) flats. Thus \(Z=\delta(a\vee b)\) has rank \(n-2\). The atoms on the line \(a\vee b\) in \(N'\) correspond under \(\delta\) to hyperplanes of \(N\) containing \(Z\), i.e. to atoms of \(\operatorname{si}(N/Z)\). The three distinct atoms \(a,b,c\) therefore give three distinct atoms of the simple rank-two matroid \(\operatorname{si}(N/Z)\). In a simple rank-two matroid, any two distinct atoms are independent and any three distinct atoms are dependent, so those three atoms form a circuit.

4. **Patch for Section 10 Plücker verification.**  
   Let \(N\) be any rank-\(n\) matroid and \(p_N\) its \(0/\infty\)-basis valuation. In a three-term Plücker relation, if no displayed sum is finite, there is nothing to prove. If one sum is finite, then the common \((n-2)\)-set \(S\) is independent, and after contracting \(S\) the assertion reduces to rank two. Suppose, in the rank-two contraction, \(\{a,b\}\) and \(\{c,d\}\) are bases. Then none of \(a,b,c,d\) is a loop, \(a\) is not parallel to \(b\), and \(c\) is not parallel to \(d\). If both cross matchings failed, then one of \(a\parallel c\) or \(b\parallel d\) would hold, and one of \(a\parallel d\) or \(b\parallel c\) would hold. Each of the four resulting combinations forces either \(a\parallel b\) or \(c\parallel d\), contradiction. Hence at least one other matching also consists of two bases, so the minimum is not uniquely finite.

   Also, in the final dependent-\(J\) step, add: if \(|J|=f\) were a basis of \(N_{F^\perp}\), then
   \[
   r_M(F^\perp\cup J)=r.
   \]
   But \(r_M(F^\perp\cup J)\le r_M(F^\perp)+r_M(J)=(r-f)+r_M(J)\), so \(r_M(J)\ge f=|J|\), forcing \(J\) to be \(M\)-independent.
problem_solved: With the patches inserted, the proof establishes the stated partial theorem: for finite simple modular \(M\) with an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\), every rank-\(k\) valuated quotient satisfying the given tropical Plücker and \(M\)-circuit-incidence relations is infinite on \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-sets and constant on bases of each rank-\(k\) flat. The formula \(p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)\) on independent \((r-k)\)-sets defines a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated quotient, descends to tropical projective classes, is involutive, and sends \(p^F\) to \(p^{F^\perp}\). It does not prove order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the full relative Dressian.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Insert references/derivations for the standard valuated-matroid facts.**  
   Use the projective tropical-linear-space convention. The inclusion/quotient equivalence is exactly: for valuated matroids \(\mu,\nu\) on the same finite ground set, \(\mu\) is a valuated quotient of \(\nu\) iff \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq\Trop(\nu)\). The incidence relations in the proof are the corresponding tropical incidence-Plücker relations. The circuit form for quotients of a constant matroid \(M\) follows by applying the circuit description of \(\Trop(M)\) to every valuated cocircuit \(C_A^*(p)\), where \(C_A^*(p)_e=p_{A\cup e}\). Since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical span of its valuated cocircuits, requiring all \(C_A^*(p)\in\Trop(M)\) is equivalent to \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\).

2. **Patch the simplification reduction.**  
   Let \(L_0\) be the loop set and let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). If \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), then for any \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\):
   - the loop circuit \(\{e\}\) forces \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\), so loops of \(M\) are loops of \(p\);
   - the circuit \(\{e,f\}\) forces \(p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}\) whenever \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), and if \(A\) contains one of \(e,f\), the relevant \(k\)-set is \(M\)-dependent and hence has value \(\infty\).
   Thus each parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(p\), or remains a zero-offset parallel class. Hence \(p\) descends to a unique valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), and conversely every quotient of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) pulls back by adding \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each simplified element by a zero-offset parallel class. This proves the claimed poset isomorphism \(\Dr(\operatorname{si}(M))\cong\Dr(M)\).

3. **Patch Lemma 5.1, Plücker part.**  
   The missing rank-three fact can be inserted as follows. Let \(P\) be a simple modular rank-three matroid and \(w\in\Trop(P)\). If \(P\) is connected, then \(P\) is a projective plane. By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant,
   \[
   w=\lambda\mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B
   \]
   for a point-line flag \(a\le B\) and \(\lambda,\mu\ge0\). For four lines \(L_1,\dots,L_4\), put \(P_{ij}=L_i\cap L_j\) when \(L_i\ne L_j\). If no \(L_i\) equals \(B\), color \(L_i\) by \(\gamma_i=L_i\cap B\). Then
   \[
   w(P_{ij})=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j=a}+\mu\mathbf 1_{\gamma_i=\gamma_j}.
   \]
   Thus the three matching sums are weighted same-color matching costs on four colored objects. By checking the possible color multiplicities \(4\), \(3+1\), \(2+2\), \(2+1+1\), and \(1+1+1+1\), the minimum is never unique. If one \(L_i=B\), each matching contains exactly one pair involving \(B\), contributing the same \(\mu\); subtract this common term and apply the same color-multiplicity check, with the \(\lambda\)-term detecting whether the other line passes through \(a\). If two \(L_i\)’s coincide, the proof’s repeated-line argument already applies. If \(P\) is disconnected, then \(P\cong U_{1,1}\oplus U_{2,n}\) or \(U_{1,1}^{\oplus3}\); in the first case the only nontrivial circuits lie on the \(U_{2,n}\)-line, and the same three-case check gives the matching relation, while in the Boolean case repetitions among the three rank-two flats force a tie.

4. **Patch Lemma 5.1, quotient part.**  
   Add the lemma: in a modular matroid \(N\), a vector \(z\) lies in \(\Trop(N)\) iff its restriction to every rank-two flat of \(N\) lies in that rank-two Bergman fan. Proof: if the rank-two conditions hold, then every upper level set \(\{e:z_e\ge t\}\) is closed under lines; in a modular geometry, line-closed sets are flats. Hence no circuit can have a unique minimum.  
   Now fix \(A\) of size \(m-1=r-k-1\), set \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), \(Y=X^\perp\), and consider
   \[
   f(c)=p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp)
   \quad(c\notin X).
   \]
   To show \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\), check a rank-two flat \(W/X\). Let \(Z=W^\perp\), a rank-\((k-1)\) flat in \(Y\). For any three distinct atoms \(R_1,R_2,R_3\) of \(W/X\), their polars \(R_i^\perp\) are the rank-\(k\) flats between \(Z\) and \(Y\). Choose a basis \(A_0\) of \(Z\) and elements \(y_i\in R_i^\perp\setminus Z\). In \((M|Y)/Z\), the \(y_i\) form a rank-two circuit, so there is an \(M\)-circuit \(D\subseteq Z\cup\{y_1,y_2,y_3\}\) whose elements outside \(Z\) are exactly \(y_1,y_2,y_3\). The quotient circuit relation for \(p\) with \(A_0\) gives
   \[
   \min_i p_{A_0\cup y_i}
   \]
   with no unique finite minimum, i.e.
   \[
   \min_i \bar p(R_i^\perp)
   \]
   with no unique finite minimum. Parallel pairs are even easier because the two corresponding polars are equal. Therefore \(f\in\Trop(M/X)\), which is precisely the circuit-incidence condition for \(p^\dagger\) to be a quotient of \(M\).

5. **Patch the polar-basis section.**  
   For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the flats generated by subsets of \(B\) form a Boolean sublattice:
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}(B_S)\wedge\operatorname{cl}(B_T)=\operatorname{cl}(B_{S\cap T}),\qquad
   \operatorname{cl}(B_S)\vee\operatorname{cl}(B_T)=\operatorname{cl}(B_{S\cup T}).
   \]
   Applying the polarity gives
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
   =
   \operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
   \]
   For \(S=\{1,\dots,r\}\), the right-hand side is \(E\), so \(B^*\) spans; for \(|S|=s\), it has rank \(s\), so \(B^*\) is independent and hence a basis. The same identity also shows that the polar-basis construction is involutive, so every basis is the polar basis of another.  
   Finally, if \(N\) is the underlying matroid of a quotient \(p\) of \(M\), every flat of \(N\) is a flat of \(M\). Thus an \(M\)-basis \(B\) cannot be contained in a proper \(N\)-flat, so \(B\) spans \(N\), and \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

6. **Patch the point-annihilator theorem.**  
   First prove the Boolean annihilator statement directly: for an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\) on \(\Omega\),
   \[
   u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
   \]
   iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical span of those cocircuits and a tropical hyperplane is tropically convex, this is equivalent to \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).  
   In the forward direction of Theorem 7.1, after checking the basis equations for \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\), also check dependent \(r\)-subsets \(J\). If \(\operatorname{rk}J<r-1\), all relevant \(\alpha(x)\)-coordinates are \(\infty\). If \(\operatorname{rk}J=r-1\), then \(J\) has a unique circuit \(C\), the finite terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and the \(\alpha(x)_{J\setminus j}\) are all equal. Since \(C_A\in\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \((C_A)_j\) on \(C\) is not unique. Thus \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\).  
   In the reverse direction, the proof’s independent-\(J\) argument via Boolean apartments is valid after adding the polar-basis patch. For dependent \(J\) of size \(r-k+1\), the same unique-circuit argument applies: if \(\operatorname{rk}J<r-k\), all terms are \(\infty\); if \(\operatorname{rk}J=r-k\), the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\)-terms occur exactly on the unique circuit \(C\subseteq J\) and are equal, while \(x\in\Trop(M)\) gives no unique minimum on \(C\).
problem_solved: After inserting the patches above and working under the standard finite-ground-set/projective tropical linear-space convention, the argument proves the claimed affirmative result: if the lattice of flats of \(M\) has an order-reversing involution, then \(\Dr(M)\), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces, has an order-reversing involution extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on the embedded flat subposet.

  > For every rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of a finite simple modular matroid \(M\) with polarity, the function \(p^\dagger\) defined by

  Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on \(B\). The valuated circuits of \(\mu^*\) are exactly the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\). Since \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits of \(\mu\), and a tropical hyperplane \(H_u\) is tropically convex, this is equivalent to \(\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).

For parallel elements \(e,f\) of \(M\), if they are separated in the underlying matroid of \(q\), a cocircuit separates their coordinates, contradicting \(x_e=x_f\) on \(\Trop(M)\). If they are valuated-parallel with offset \(\delta\), the two-element circuit equation forces \(x_e-x_f=\delta\) on \(\Trop(q)\), so containment in \(x_e=x_f\) forces \(\delta=0\). Then \(q\) descends uniquely by choosing one representative per parallel class.

At an adjacent step \(B'=B-b+f\), if \(b\notin I\), keep \(I\). If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I-b\). The fundamental circuit of \(f\) with respect to \(B\) contains \(b\), and the circuit-incidence condition for \(p\) applied to \(A\) gives some \(c\neq b\) in that circuit with \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\). Then \(A\cup c\subseteq B'\) is the new finite \(p\)-basis.

- **Patch for flat quotient complements:** For \(p^F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), an \(M\)-basis \(I\) of a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}F\) flat \(G\) has finite \(p^F_I\) iff \(I\cap F=\varnothing\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(F\vee G)=r\). problem_solved: The proof establishes, modulo routine details, that the flat-lattice involution forces the simplification of \(M\) to be modular; it defines a formal dagger operation on valuated quotients in the simple modular case; it proves involutivity of this formal operation as a function-level construction; and it sketches an annihilator argument that would yield order reversal if \(p^\dagger\) were known to be a valuated matroid quotient.    - For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on finite \(E\) of ranks \(k\le \ell\), \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) iff \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q)\), equivalently the tropical incidence relations hold.

2. **Simplification patch.** If \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\) and \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Applying the circuit quotient relation to \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\) gives a minimum over the singleton \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\); hence \(p_I=\infty\). For a parallel pair \(e,f\), the circuit \(\{e,f\}\) gives \(p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}\) for all \(A\cap\{e,f\}=\varnothing\), with both values \(\infty\) if the class becomes looped. Thus \(p\) descends uniquely to the simplification, and conversely every quotient of the simplification pulls back by adding loops and zero-offset parallel classes. This proves the claimed \(\Delta:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(M)\) poset isomorphism, with the all-loop rank-zero case handled separately as the one-element Dressian \(\{\varnothing\}\).

3. **Lattice patch.** In a finite geometric lattice, an order-reversing involution sends intervals \([\hat0,F]\) to \([F^\perp,\hat1]\), so \(\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F)\). Applying semimodularity to \(X,Y\) and to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives both inequalities, hence modularity. In the modular case, for a basis \(B\), the flats \(\operatorname{cl}(B_S)\) form a Boolean sublattice because modularity gives
   \[
   \rho(\operatorname{cl}(B_S)\wedge\operatorname{cl}(B_T))=|S\cap T|.
   \]
   Also, if \(N\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(N\)-flat is an \(M\)-flat; therefore no \(M\)-basis is contained in a proper \(N\)-flat, so every \(M\)-basis spans \(N\).

4. **Rank-two testing patch.** In Lemma 5.1, first pass to the simplification of \(N\); the hypothesis that \(z\) is constant on parallel classes makes this legitimate. For each real \(t\), \(U_t=\{e:z_e\ge t\}\) satisfies the circuit condition iff it is a flat: if \(U_t\) is not closed, there is a circuit with exactly one element outside \(U_t\), giving a unique finite minimum; conversely a unique minimum in a circuit gives such a nonclosed upper set. The proof that line-closed atom sets are flats in a modular geometric lattice is correct once one notes that a minimal atom set spanning \(e\) is independent, so the displayed rank calculation is valid.

5. **Rank-three matching patch.** In Lemma 5.2, justify the structural alternatives for a simple modular rank-three matroid: disconnected cases are \(U_{1,1}^{\oplus3}\) or \(U_{1,1}\oplus U_{2,n}\); in the connected case any two distinct lines meet in a point. For \(w\in\Trop(P)\), after subtracting \(\min w\), the upper-level sets form a chain of flats, so in rank three
   \[
   w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu\mathbf 1_B
   \]
   for a point-line flag \(a\le B\), allowing \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\). The color-counting argument then works; if \(\mu=\infty\) or \(\lambda=\infty\), the relevant sums are either all \(\infty\) or still have at least two finite minima.

   - The checked three-term tropical Plücker relations are accepted as the valuated-matroid axiom; equivalently cite the standard equivalence with valuated basis exchange.

Hence a unique minimum on \(C\setminus X\) would contradict the Bergman condition for \(f\).

Modularity gives the meet condition from the rank count. Polarity swaps joins and meets, so complements of \(F\) correspond exactly to complements of \(F^\perp\).

8. **Theorem 8.1 boundary patch.** For \(k=0\), \(p^\dagger=M\), so the statement is tautological. For \(k=r\), \(p=M\) and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so \(\Trop(p^\dagger)=\varnothing\). To show the right side is also false, choose \(e\) with \(x_e<\infty\), extend \(e\) to a polar basis \(B^*\), and let \(B=(B^*)^*\). PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Quotient/circuit-incidence criterion.**  
For a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(p\), its valuated cocircuits are
\[
C_A(e)=p_{A\cup e},\qquad |A|=k-1.
\]
For an ordinary matroid \(M\),
\[
\Trop(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}
\{x:\min_{c\in C}x_c\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of the valuated cocircuits of \(p\), and each circuit hyperplane of \(M\) is tropically convex, \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\) is equivalent to requiring every cocircuit \(C_A\) to satisfy every \(M\)-circuit equation:
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. This is exactly the circuit-incidence condition.

2. **Support quotient.**  
Let \(N=\operatorname{supp}(p)\). The support-level circuit-incidence condition implies that every \(M\)-circuit is a union of \(N\)-circuits. Indeed, if some \(e\in C\) were a coloop of \(N|C\), choose an \(N\)-hyperplane \(H\) containing \(C-e\) but not \(e\), and choose an \(N\)-basis \(A\) of \(H\). Then \(A\cup e\) is an \(N\)-basis, while \(A\cup c\) is \(N\)-dependent for all \(c\in C-e\), contradicting circuit-incidence. Hence \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), equivalently
\[
r_N(A)=r_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\]
for all \(A\subseteq E\).

3. **Simplification reduction.**  
If \(e\) is an \(M\)-loop, apply circuit-incidence to the circuit \(\{e\}\); this forces \(p_{A\cup e}=\infty\) for all \(A\), so \(e\) is a loop of every quotient \(p\). If \(e,f\) are \(M\)-parallel, apply circuit-incidence to \(\{e,f\}\). For every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) disjoint from \(\{e,f\}\),
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f}.
\]
Thus a quotient is constant on each nonloop parallel class, with zero valuated offset, or the whole class becomes loops. This gives a well-defined quotient on the simplification, and conversely the zero-parallel extension of a quotient of \(\operatorname{si}(M)\) is a quotient of \(M\). Therefore the map \(\Delta:\Dr(\operatorname{si}(M))\to\Dr(M)\) is a poset isomorphism.

4. **Rank function \(\rho^\dagger\).**  
After proving \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), the inequality
\[
r_N(G^\perp)-r_N(H^\perp)
\le r_M(G^\perp)-r_M(H^\perp)
\]
for \(G\subseteq H\) follows from the strong-map quotient property. For arbitrary subsets \(A,B\), apply the flat inequality to \(G=\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), \(H=\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\), and use
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B)=G\vee H,\qquad
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cap B)\subseteq G\wedge H.
\]
This gives submodularity for all subsets. Also \(\rho^\dagger(\emptyset)=0\), monotonicity gives nonnegativity, and
\[
\rho^\dagger(A)\le r_M(A)\le |A|.
\]
Submodularity plus \(\rho^\dagger(\{e\})\le1\) gives
\[
\rho^\dagger(A\cup e)-\rho^\dagger(A)\le1.
\]
Thus \(\rho^\dagger\) is a matroid rank function.

5. **Superlevel-flat criterion.**  
For a vector \(w\) on a simple matroid \(N\),
\[
w\in\Trop(N)
\]
iff every superlevel set \(\{e:w_e\ge\lambda\}\) is a flat. If a superlevel set is not flat, some circuit has a unique element below level \(\lambda\), giving a unique minimum. Conversely, a circuit with unique minimum produces a non-flat superlevel set just above that minimum.

6. **Line-closed sets in modular geometries.**  
Let \(G\) be a simple modular matroid and \(S\) a line-closed set of points. Prove \(S\) is a flat by induction on \(r(\operatorname{cl}S)\). Choose a basis \(a_1,\dots,a_m\subseteq S\), let \(H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1})\), and use induction to get all points of \(H\) in \(S\). For any point \(x\in\operatorname{cl}(S)\setminus H\), modularity of the hyperplane \(H\) implies the line \(\operatorname{cl}(a_m,x)\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). Since \(h,a_m\in S\) and \(S\) is line-closed, \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is flat.

7. **Contraction circuit step in Section 8.**  
If \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), set \(D=C\setminus X\). For each \(c\in D\),
\[
r_{M/X}(D-c)
= r_M(X\cup C-c)-r_M(X)
= r_M(X\cup C)-r_M(X)
= r_{M/X}(D),
\]
because \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\). Thus \(c\) is not a coloop of \((M/X)|D\), so \(c\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\). Hence a unique minimum of \(u\) on \(D\) would restrict to a unique minimum on one such circuit, contradiction.

8. **Ordinary valuated annihilator theorem.**  
For an ordinary valuated matroid \(\mu\),
\[
u\in\Trop(\mu^*)
\iff
u\perp C\text{ for every valuated cocircuit }C\text{ of }\mu
\iff
\Trop(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
The first equivalence is the circuit description of \(\Trop(\mu^*)\), since cocircuits of \(\mu\) are circuits of \(\mu^*\). The second follows because \(\Trop(\mu)\) is the tropical convex hull of the cocircuits of \(\mu\), and a tropical hyperplane \(H_u\) is tropically convex.

9. **Apartment verification in Section 11.**  
For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_i\}\), prove explicitly that
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Then \(B^*\) is a basis and \((B^*)^*=B\), so \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases. Restricting \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) gives the ordinary dual \(\mu_B^*\). The dependent \(r\)-set and \((r-k+1)\)-set cases reduce to ordinary \(M\)-circuit equations because the only finite Plücker terms occur on the unique circuit of the dependent set; if the common coefficient is \(\infty\), the equation is trivial.

10. **Edge cases.**  
If \(k=0\) or \(k=r\), the relevant Plücker or quotient conditions are vacuous or reduce to the unique rank \(0\) or rank \(r\) quotient. If an auxiliary vector \(w\) or \(u\) is identically \(\infty\), all minima in the relevant local relations have no unique finite minimizer, so the local arguments are trivial.

   r_N(B)-r_N(A)\le r_M(B)-r_M(A)\qquad(A\subseteq B)

   \bar p(X)<\infty \iff r_N(X)=k.

   Define, for \(S\subseteq E\) and \(X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\),
   \[
   r^\dagger(S):=\rho_M(X)-k+r_N(X^\perp).
   \]
   This is a matroid rank function. Nonnegativity follows from the quotient rank inequality:
   \[
   k-r_N(X^\perp)\le r-\rho(X^\perp)=\rho(X).
   \]
   Also \(r^\dagger(S)\le \rho(X)\le |S|\). Monotonicity follows from the same quotient rank inequality. For submodularity, for \(M\)-flats \(X,Y\), put \(A=X^\perp\), \(B=Y^\perp\). Modularity of \(M\) cancels the \(\rho\)-terms, and De Morgan gives
   \[
   (X\vee Y)^\perp=A\cap B,\qquad

   r_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup B))=r_N(A\cup B).
   \]

   r_N(A)+r_N(B)\ge r_N(A\cap B)+r_N(A\cup B).

   p^\dagger_J=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty.
   \]

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Standard valuated-matroid facts.** Add citations to Brandt–Eur–Zhang for quotient-containment and to a cocircuit-span reference for uniqueness/cocircuit generation. Also insert the following proof of the circuit-incidence criterion: if \(p\) is rank \(k\) and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), then each coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), so every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) satisfies  
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Conversely, if these conditions hold for all \(A,C\), then all coordinate cocircuits of \(p\) lie in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\); since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of those cocircuits and \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) is closed tropically convex, containment follows.

2. **Superlevel-flat criterion.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\), prove: \(w\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\) iff every \(\{e:w_e\ge\lambda\}\) is a flat. If a superlevel set \(A\) is not flat, choose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(A)\setminus A\); then some circuit \(C\subseteq A\cup\{e\}\) contains \(e\), and for \(\lambda>w_e\) with \(A=\{w\ge\lambda\}\), \(e\) is the unique finite minimum on \(C\). Conversely, if a circuit has unique finite minimum at \(e\), choose \(\lambda\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest circuit value; then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq\{w\ge\lambda\}\) but \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(C\setminus\{e\})\), so the superlevel set is not flat.

3. **Descent through parallel classes.** If \(e,f\) are parallel in \(M\), equality \(C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f)\) for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\) implies: no finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\); if \(I\ni e\) is finite and \(f\notin I\), then with \(A=I\setminus\{e\}\) one gets \(q_I=q_{I-e+f}\). Thus each original parallel class is either looped in \(q\) or becomes a zero-offset valuated parallel class. This makes \(\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I\) well-defined and the Plücker relations descend by choosing representatives.

4. **Lemma 3.1 codimension-four case.** In the interval \([Z,Y]\), the rank is \(4\). For three distinct hyperplanes, their meet has codimension at most \(3\), hence \(r(T_i)\ge k-1\). If \(r(T_i)=k\), then meeting with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) gives rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(r(Z)=k-2\). Hence \(r(T_i)=k-1\). Then \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\), and modularity gives
\[
r(T_m\vee T_n)=r(T_m)+r(T_n)-r(Z)=k.
\]
Since \(T_m\vee T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j\) and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) also has rank \(k\), equality follows.

5. **Circuit lifting.** If \(D\) is a circuit of the simplification of a contraction/restriction and one representative is chosen from each parallel class in \(D\), the representative set is a circuit in the unsimplified contraction. By the standard contraction-circuit description, this lifts to an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}\) with \(C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}\). This justifies the circuit-incidence applications in Lemmas 3.1 and 4.1.

6. **Line-closed implies flat in modular matroids.** Complete Lemma 4.1 by choosing a maximal independent subset \(B\) of a line-closed set \(S\). The induction in the proof shows every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). Maximality gives \(S\subseteq\operatorname{cl}(B)\), so \(S\) is exactly the point set of a flat.

7. **Boolean-apartment identity.** For \(F_S=\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\in S\}\), modularity gives
\[
r(F_S\wedge F_T)=r(F_S)+r(F_T)-r(F_S\vee F_T)=|S|+|T|-|S\cup T|=|S\cap T|.
\]
Since \(F_{S\cap T}\subseteq F_S\wedge F_T\) with the same rank, equality holds. Iterating gives
\[
F_S=\bigwedge_{i\notin S}F_{[r]\setminus\{i\}}.
\]
Applying the polarity gives the stated formula for \(B^*\), and then \(B^*\) is a basis with \((B^*)^*=B\).

8. **Ordinary tropical annihilator duality.** For rank \(k\) \(\mu\), the circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are exactly the orthogonality conditions between \(u\) and the coordinate cocircuits \(C_A^\mu\), because
\[
(\mu^*)_{T\setminus i}=\mu_{N\setminus(T\setminus i)}
\]
with \(A=N\setminus T\). Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\) iff \(H_u\) contains every coordinate cocircuit of \(\mu\). Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of these cocircuits and \(H_u\) is closed tropically convex, this is equivalent to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).

9. **Flat quotient complement criterion.** For \(p^F\) the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), a rank-\(r-r(F)\) flat \(G\) has \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) iff a basis \(I\) of \(G\) is independent in \(M/F\), i.e. \(r_M(F\cup I)=r(M)\), so \(F\vee G=E\). Modularity then gives
\[
r(F\wedge G)=r(F)+r(G)-r(E)=0,
\]
so \(G\) is a complement of \(F\). The converse is immediate.

   \[
   \rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
   \]
   Since \(L\cong L^{\mathrm{op}}\), the opposite lattice is also semimodular. Applying the same inequality in \(L^{\mathrm{op}}\), whose rank is \(r-\rho\), gives the reverse inequality. 2. **Patch the Plücker sufficiency step.** Before Section 4.3’s verification, state:

   > A nonzero vector \(v\in\mathbb T^{\binom{E}{d}}\) is a rank-\(d\) valuated matroid iff it satisfies the three-term tropical Plücker relations
   > \[
   > \min(v_{Sij}+v_{Skl},\,v_{Sik}+v_{Sjl},\,v_{Sil}+v_{Sjk})
   > \]
   > with no unique finite minimizer for all \(S\in\binom{E}{d-2}\) and distinct \(i,j,k,l\notin S\).

   The proof already shows \(p^\dagger\not\equiv\infty\) for \(0<k<r\), treats \(k=0,r\) separately, and checks precisely these relations.

3. **Patch the modular-lattice rank details in Lemma 4.1.** Add:

   In a modular lattice, if \(Y\) has rank \(m\) and \(H\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), then for any \(A\le Y\),
   \[
   \rho(A\wedge H)=\rho(A)+\rho(H)-\rho(A\vee H)\ge \rho(A)-1.
   \]
   Therefore the meet of four hyperplanes of a rank-\((k+2)\) flat has rank at least \(k-2\). If \(Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4\) has rank \(k-2\), then each triple meet
   \[
   T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j
   \]
   has rank \(k-1\): it has rank at least \(k-1\), and if it had rank \(k\), intersecting with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) could lower rank by at most \(1\), giving \(\rho(Z)\ge k-1\), contradiction.

4. **Patch circuit lifting.** Insert the standard lemma:

   > Let \(X\) be a flat of \(M\). If \(D\) is a circuit of the simplification of \(M/X\) and one representative \(e_P\) is chosen from each parallel class \(P\in D\), then there exists an \(M\)-circuit
   > \[
   > C\subseteq X\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
   > \]
   > with \(C\setminus X=\{e_P:P\in D\}\).

   Proof: the chosen representatives form a circuit of \(M/X\). By the circuit description of contraction, some \(M\)-circuit \(C\) has \(C\setminus X\) contained in those representatives. Minimality of \(D\) in the simplification forces equality.

   Also add: if \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit not contained in \(X\), then every \(e\in C\setminus X\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\). Indeed,
   \[
   e\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{e\})\subseteq
   \operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\})),
   \]
   so \(e\) is dependent on \(C\setminus X\setminus\{e\}\) in \(M/X\), and a minimal dependent subset gives the desired circuit.

5. **Patch Section 2.3 wording.** Replace the flat description by:

   \[
   F\in\mathcal L(M)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
   \text{ for a flat }\bar F\in\mathcal L(\operatorname{si}(M)).
   \]
   Then \(\psi(F)=\bar F\) is a lattice isomorphism, and the displayed rank computation proves compatibility of flat quotients.
problem_solved: Modulo the listed standard valuated-matroid facts and routine matroid patches, the proof establishes the finite-ground-set version: if a finite matroid \(M\) has an order-reversing involution on its lattice of flats, then the relative Dressian of tropical linear subspaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) admits an inclusion-reversing involution extending \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on the embedded flat-quotient objects.

For example, in rank \(3\) on \(\{1,\dots,6\}\), the function finite only on \(123\) and \(456\) satisfies all short 3-term relations vacuously, but its support is not a matroid basis set. The proof never proves that the finite \(p^\dagger\)-bases form a matroid, nor does it verify full valuated basis exchange/full tropical Plücker relations. PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
**Major missing lemma needed.** Replace Fact 1 by the correct criterion:

> A function \(q:\binom E d\to\mathbb T\), not identically \(\infty\), is a valuated matroid iff its finite support \(\{I:q_I<\infty\}\) is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid and the finite values satisfy the valuated basis-exchange axiom; equivalently, one may verify the full tropical Plücker relations.

To repair Section 5, one must additionally prove for the constructed \(p^\dagger\) that
\[
\mathcal B^\dagger
=
\{J\in\binom E{r-k}: J\text{ is }M\text{-independent and }
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of a matroid, and that the values \(p^\dagger_J\) satisfy full valuated basis exchange. The current proof supplies only the local 3-term relations, which is insufficient. I do not see a complete local patch in the given argument; this requires a new nontrivial argument.

**Routine patch: contraction circuit lifting.** If \(X\) is a flat and \(D\) is a circuit of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\), choose representatives \(e_P\) in the corresponding parallel classes of \(M/X\). The representative set is dependent in \(M/X\), and no proper subset meeting fewer classes is dependent by minimality of \(D\). Hence it contains a circuit \(D'\) of \(M/X\) meeting exactly those classes. By the standard description of circuits of a contraction, there is an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq X\cup D'\) with \(C\setminus X=D'\). This is the lifting used in Lemmas 5.2 and 6.1.

**Routine patch: modularity under contraction/simplification.** For a flat \(X\), the lattice of flats of \(M/X\) is the interval \([X,E]\) in \(\mathcal L(M)\). If \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, then every interval is modular. Simplification does not change the lattice of flats except for replacing parallel classes by single points. Therefore \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

**Routine patch: \(B^*\)-involution.** From
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B_S)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\},
\]
taking \(S=\{i\}\) gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})
=
\operatorname{cl}_M(\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Applying \(\perp\) again gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})^\perp=\operatorname{cl}_M(b_i),
\]
so, since \(M\) is simple, the star of \(b_i^*\) relative to \(B^*\) is \(b_i\). Thus \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution. Consequently, as \(B\) ranges over bases, \(B^*\) also ranges over bases, so the elements \(b_i^*\) range over all nonloops, i.e. all of \(E\) in the simple loopless case.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch for Facts 1.1–1.4.** Insert the standard covector theorem: for a rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(p\), the affine tropical linear space \(L(p)\) equals the tropical convex span/submodule generated by its coordinate cocircuits \(D_A^p\), \(|A|=d-1\). This is a standard Murota–Tamura/Dress–Wenzel valuated-matroid fact; modern references state that tropical linear spaces are spanned by valuated cocircuits and that valuated matroid quotients are equivalent to inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces. ([pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12031988/?utm_source=openai))

   Then Fact 1.1 follows as follows. For each ordinary \(M\)-circuit \(C\), let
   \[
   H_C=\{x:\min_{c\in C}x_c\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
   \]
   Each \(H_C\) is tropically convex: if a tropical linear combination of two vectors in \(H_C\) had a unique finite minimum at \(e\in C\), whichever summand attained that value at \(e\) would itself have a unique finite minimum on \(C\). Since
   \[
   L(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)}H_C,
   \]
   containment \(L(p)\subseteq L(M)\) is equivalent, by the cocircuit-span theorem, to every \(D_A^p\) lying in every \(H_C\), exactly the incidence condition in Fact 1.1. Fact 1.2 is the same covector theorem. Fact 1.3 follows because circuits of \(\mu^*\) are cocircuits of \(\mu\), so \(u\in L(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every cocircuit generator of \(L(\mu)\), equivalently to all of \(L(\mu)\). Fact 1.4 can either be cited as the standard uniqueness of a valuated matroid from its covector/tropical-linear-space module, or removed if unused.

2. **Patch for Section 2.** Add the explicit verification: the zero-parallel extension \(q\) has finite bases exactly the representative sets of finite \(\bar q\)-bases with no loops and no repeated parallel class. Any tropical Plücker relation for \(q\) either projects to the corresponding relation for \(\bar q\), or contains a loop/repeated parallel class and has all finite terms paired with equal values, hence is tautological. For tropical linear spaces, equations involving loops force loop coordinates to be \(\infty\), and equations involving two representatives of a nonloop parallel class force those coordinates to be equal; after imposing these conditions, the remaining equations are precisely those of \(\bar q\). Thus
   \[
   \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)=\Delta(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)).
   \]

3. **Patch for Proposition 8.2.** State explicitly that if \(B\) is an \(M\)-basis and \(B\) contains a finite \(p\)-basis, then
   \[
   \mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}
   \]
   is the deletion/restriction of \(p\) to \(B\), hence is a rank \(k\) valuated matroid. If \(y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), then \(y|_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B)\) because all defining circuit equations for \(\mu_B\) are defining equations for \(p\) restricted to subsets of \(B\). The same applies to \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}\). Finally, note explicitly that adding a tropical scalar to \(x\), \(y\), \(p\), or \(p^\dagger\) does not affect any “no unique finite minimizer” condition, so the affine construction descends to projective tropical space.
problem_solved: After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the following conditional finite result: for a finite matroid \(M\) whose flat lattice has an order-reversing involution, one may reduce canonically to the simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), whose flat lattice is modular. In the finite simple loopless modular case with polarity, the construction \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is defined for every valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), is nonzero and formally involutive, sends flat quotients indexed by \(F\) to those indexed by \(F^\perp\), and satisfies the stated local short three-term relations and \(M\)-circuit incidence relations. Assuming the additional dagger hypothesis that every such \(p^\dagger\) is a genuine valuated matroid, the construction gives an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the polarity on flats. The proof does not establish the dagger hypothesis and therefore does not solve the original unconditional problem.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch for ordinary tropical annihilator duality.**  
   Let \(\mu\) be rank \(k\) on finite \(R\). For \(A\in\binom{R}{k-1}\), define the valuated cocircuit
   \[
   D_A^\mu(i)=
   \begin{cases}
   \mu_{A\cup i},& i\notin A,\\
   \infty,& i\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   The circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) say that for every \(J\in\binom{R}{|R|-k+1}\),
   \[
   \min_{j\in J}\bigl(u_j+\mu^*_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer. With \(A=R\setminus J\), this is exactly
   \[
   \min_{j\in R}\bigl(u_j+D_A^\mu(j)\bigr)
   \]
   having no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)\) iff \(u\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit \(D_A^\mu\). Since \(\widetilde L(\mu)\) is the closed tropical span of its valuated cocircuits and tropical hyperplanes are tropically convex and closed, this is equivalent to \(\widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u\).

2. **Patch for rank reversal and modularity.**  
   In a finite ranked lattice, an order-reversing involution sends maximal chains to maximal chains, hence sends rank \(i\) elements to rank \(r-i\) elements. Therefore \(\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F)\). Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives
   \[
   \rho(X)+\rho(Y)\le \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
   \]
   while semimodularity for \(X,Y\) gives the reverse inequality. Hence equality holds for all \(X,Y\), so the lattice is modular.

3. **Patch that \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(r-k\).**  
   Since \(p\) has rank \(k\), choose a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\). Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Then \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). If \(J\) is an \(M\)-basis of \(F^\perp\), then
   \[
   p^\dagger_J=\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)=\bar p(F)=p_I<\infty.
   \]
   Thus \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\) and has the claimed rank.

4. **Patch for the local dual Plücker lemma.**  
   Let \(Y\) have rank \(k+2\), let \(H_1,\dots,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), and set \(Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4\). If two \(H_i\)’s coincide, the asserted relation is immediate because one bracket is \(\infty\) and the other two sums agree. Otherwise modularity gives \(\operatorname{rk}Z\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}\).

   - If \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) has rank \(k\) and contains \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). All three Plücker sums are equal.
   - If \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\), work in the rank-four interval \([Z,Y]\). For each \(i\), choose an atom \(e_i\) below \(\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j\) but not below \(H_i\). Then \(e_1,\dots,e_4\) are independent over \(Z\), because \(e_i\) is not in the join of the other three, which lies in \(H_i\). Hence, after choosing a basis \(A\) of \(Z\),
     \[
     H_i\wedge H_j=Z\vee e_\ell\vee e_m
     \]
     where \(\{\ell,m\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\setminus\{i,j\}\). Thus \([ij]=p_{A\cup e_\ell\cup e_m}\), and the desired relation is exactly the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A,e_1,e_2,e_3,e_4\).
   - If \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\), pass to the rank-three simple modular matroid \(N=\operatorname{si}(Y/Z)\). The function \(w(P)=\bar p(Z\vee P)\) on points \(P\) of \(N\) lies in \(\widetilde L(N)\), because it is the restriction of the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) for a basis \(A\) of \(Z\). By the Bergman criterion, the superlevel sets of \(w\) are flats. In rank three, this means \(w\), up to an additive constant, has the form
     \[
     \alpha\mathbf 1_L+\beta\mathbf 1_P
     \]
     for a flag \(P\le L\), with \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), allowing \(\infty\). For four lines \(\ell_i=H_i/Z\), the three matching sums count, with weights \(\alpha,\beta\), which matched intersections lie on \(L\) and which equal \(P\). A direct enumeration of the equality patterns of four points on a projective line gives count vectors of type \((0,0,0)\), \((1,0,0)\), \((2,0,0)\), \((1,1,1)\), or \((2,2,2)\), up to permutation; none has a unique minimum, and the same remains true after adding the point-flag contribution \(\beta\mathbf 1_P\). Therefore the local Plücker relation holds.

5. **Patch for the codimension-one polarity lemma.**  
   Let \(X\) have rank \(n-1\), and let \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) be points of \(\operatorname{si}(M/X)\) with \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\). Put \(H_i=P_i^\perp\subset X^\perp\). Then \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are hyperplanes of \(X^\perp\), and \(H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2\). Let \(A\) be a basis of \(H_1\wedge H_2\), and choose representatives \(h_i\in H_i\setminus(H_1\wedge H_2)\). In the contraction by \(H_1\wedge H_2\), the three \(h_i\)’s form a rank-two circuit. Lifting gives an \(M\)-circuit contained in \(A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\). Circuit-incidence for \(p\) yields that
   \[
   \min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer. Hence every superlevel set of \(u\) is closed under lines. In a finite simple modular matroid, line-closed sets are flats: this follows either from Birkhoff’s classification of modular geometric lattices as products of projective geometries, where line-closed point sets are exactly projective subspaces, or by the standard atomistic modular-lattice induction. Therefore \(u\in\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))\).

6. **Patch for the point-annihilator theorem.**  
   For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), define \(\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}}\). The earlier basis-graph argument shows \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). Since
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
   =
   \operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp},
   \]
   one has
   \[
   p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in T\}}
   =
   p_{\{b_i:i\notin T\}}
   =
   \mu_B^*(T).
   \]
   Thus \(p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*\).

   If \(x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\) and \(y\in\widetilde L(p)\), then \(x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*)\) and \(y|_B\in\widetilde L(\mu_B)\), so the ordinary annihilator lemma gives the defining inequality for \(\widetilde H_x\).

   Conversely, assume \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). For each \(B\) and each \(A\subseteq B\) of size \(k-1\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives exactly the cocircuit equations for \(x|_{B^*}\in\widetilde L(\mu_B^*)\). Hence \(x\) satisfies all \(p^\dagger\)-equations supported in every Boolean apartment.

   To check an arbitrary circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\), let \(J\) have size \(r-k+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\), write \(C=B^*\), and use the apartment calculation for \(B\). If \(J\) is dependent and every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise \(J\) has rank \(|J|-1\) and contains a unique circuit \(C_0\); the only finite terms occur for \(j\in C_0\), and for those \(j\) the flats \(\operatorname{cl}(J\setminus j)\) are all equal. Thus the equation reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) on \(C_0\).

7. **Patch for boundary rank \(k=r\).**  
   If \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), then \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero, so \(\widetilde L(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}\). Suppose \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) is not all \(\infty\), and choose an element \(e\) with \(x_e<\infty\). Pick an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) with \(b_1^*=e\). Let \(H=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_1)\), and choose \(A\gg0\). Define \(y=0\) off \(H\) and \(y=A\) on \(H\). Its superlevel sets are flats, so \(y\in\widetilde L(M)\). For \(A\) large enough,
   \[
   y_{b_1}+x_{b_1^*}
   \]
   is the unique finite minimum among \(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\). Hence \(\widetilde L(M)\not\subseteq\widetilde H_x\). Therefore the only \(x\) with \(\widetilde L(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x\) is \(x=\infty^E\).

8. **Patch for projectivization and well-definedness.**  
   The affine cones used are invariant under adding constants to finite coordinates, and \(\widetilde H_{x+\lambda\mathbf 1}=\widetilde H_x\). The intrinsic formula
   \[
   \widetilde L(p^\dagger)
   =
   \{x\in\widetilde L(M):\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}
   \]
   depends only on the affine cone \(\widetilde L(p)\), not on the chosen valuated Plücker vector representing it. Therefore the construction descends to projective tropical linear spaces and is well-defined on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

The proof does not establish an infinite-ground-set version.

Conversely, if a superlevel set \(S\) is not flat, choose \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(S)\setminus S\) and a circuit \(C\subseteq S\cup\{e\}\) containing \(e\); then \(e\) is the unique minimum on \(C\).

   \[
   \widetilde L(M)=\bigcap_{C\in\mathcal C(M)} H_{u^C},
   \]
   Lemma 1.1 gives \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq H_{u^C}\) iff \(u^C\in \widetilde L(p^*)\), which is exactly
   \[
   \min_{c\in C}D_A^p(c)
   \]
   having no unique finite minimizer for every \(|A|=k-1\).

3. **Zero-parallel extension in Lemma 2.1.**  
   Prove it by adding one loop or one parallel copy at a time. A loop extension forces the new coordinate to be \(\infty\) by the equation on \(B\cup\{e\}\), where \(B\) is any finite basis. A parallel extension of \(e\) by \(f\) has
   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},\qquad q_{A\cup e\cup f}=\infty,
   \]
   so the equation on \(A\cup\{e,f\}\) forces \(x_e=x_f\). After imposing those equalities and loop coordinates, all remaining equations are precisely those of the simplified valuated matroid. Conversely, the loop/parallel circuit conditions used in the proof show that any quotient \(q\) of \(M\) has loops on \(L_0\), no finite basis containing two representatives of one parallel class, and values independent of representative choice; hence \(q\) descends to \(\bar q\), whose Plücker relations are inherited by choosing representatives.

4. **Representatives in Lemma 5.3.**  
   In the \(\rho(Z)=k-2\) case, replace each atom \(e_i\) of \([Z,Y]\) by a rank-one flat \(Q_i\) over \(Z\), and choose \(h_i\in Q_i\setminus Z\). Then \(A\cup h_\ell\cup h_m\) is a basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\), so
   \[
   [ij]=p_{A\cup h_\ell\cup h_m}.
   \]
   The desired relation is the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A,h_1,h_2,h_3,h_4\).  
   In the \(\rho(Z)=k-1\) case, \(w(P)=\bar p(Z\vee P)\) is well-defined by flat constancy. For a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq\widetilde L(M)\), has value \(\infty\) on \(Z\), and descends to \(Y/Z\). Its superlevel sets descend to flats of \(\operatorname{si}(Y/Z)\), so \(w\in\widetilde L(N)\).

5. **Circuit lifting in Lemma 5.4.**  
   Since \(h_1,h_2,h_3\) are three distinct points on a rank-two line in \(M/W\), they form a circuit in \(M/W\). With \(A\) a basis of \(W\), the set \(A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\) is dependent, while \(A\cup\{h_i,h_j\}\) is independent for \(i\ne j\). Therefore any circuit contained in \(A\cup\{h_1,h_2,h_3\}\) contains all three \(h_i\), giving the circuit needed for quotient-incidence.

6. **Dual basis proof in Section 7.**  
   For \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\), containment
   \[
   \bigvee_{i\in S} b_i^*\le \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S)^\perp
   \]
   is immediate. To prove equality, show independence: if \(j\notin T\) and
   \[
   b_j^*\le \bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*,
   \]
   then applying \(\perp\) gives
   \[
   \left(\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*\right)^\perp\le (b_j^*)^\perp=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_j).
   \]
   But the known containment for \(T\) gives
   \[
   \left(\bigvee_{i\in T}b_i^*\right)^\perp\ge \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T),
   \]
   and \(b_j\in \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus T)\), contradiction. Thus the \(b_i^*\) are independent, both sides have rank \(|S|\), and equality follows. Then \(B^*\) is a basis and \((B^*)^*=B\).

7. **Flat quotients.**  
   For \(\rho(G)=r-\rho(F)\),
   \[
   \bar p^F(G)<\infty
   \]
   iff a basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), iff \(F\vee G=E\). By modularity and the rank sum,
   \[
   F\vee G=E \iff F\wedge G=\hat 0.
   \]
   Thus finite bases correspond exactly to lattice complements of \(F\). The polarity swaps complements of \(F\) with complements of \(F^\perp\), so the finite supports and values of \((p^F)^\dagger\) and \(p^{F^\perp}\) agree.

8. **Degenerate rank.**  
   If the simplified matroid has rank \(0\), its ground set is empty and \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) has only the rank-zero projective space. Define \(\Phi\) to be the identity; the flat-lattice condition is automatic.

It does not establish any infinite-ground-set version.
